file_name: Maurya et al. - 2024 - Basal cell carcinoma diagnosis with fusion of deep learning and telangiectasia features.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically U-Net for semantic segmentation of telangiectasia and EfficientNet-B5 for feature extraction from whole lesion images, combined with an ensemble learning classifier (Random Forest) to perform binary classification of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) vs non-BCC in dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel fusion technique for Basal Cell Carcinoma diagnosis using deep learning (U-Net, EfficientNet-B5) and handcrafted telangiectasia features. It describes specific methods, datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2019, NIH), and reports new quantitative results (97.2% accuracy, AUC 0.99) on a holdout test set, distinguishing it from a review or survey.

title: Basal Cell Carcinoma Diagnosis with Fusion of Deep Learning and Telangiectasia Features
journal: Journal of Imaging Informatics in Medicine
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel fusion technique for binary classification of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) versus non-BCC in dermoscopy images by combining deep learning features with handcrafted telangiectasia features. The methodology employs a U-Net model for semantic segmentation of telangiectasia and an EfficientNet-B5 model for feature extraction from whole lesion images, which are then integrated using a Random Forest ensemble classifier. The model was trained and evaluated on a dataset comprising 1000 BCC images and 1000 non-BCC images sourced from the HAM10000, ISIC 2019, and NIH datasets. Evaluation on a holdout test set of 395 images demonstrated state-of-the-art performance, achieving a binary classification accuracy of 97.2% and an AUC of 0.99. The fusion approach yielded a 1.3% improvement in accuracy over the deep learning-only model, along with increases in sensitivity, specificity, and precision. These results indicate that incorporating clinically observable vascular features enhances diagnostic accuracy and explainability in BCC detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the binary classification task. The study used 1000 BCC images sourced from HAM10000, ISIC 2019, and NIH datasets.
The negative class in the binary classification task, comprising 1000 images from five specific benign categories.
One of the five benign categories included in the non-BCC dataset, representing common benign skin conditions.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Basal Cell Carcinoma vs. non-Basal Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of dermoscopy images into BCC or non-BCC classes using a fusion of deep learning features (EfficientNet-B5) and handcrafted telangiectasia features (U-Net segmentation) fed into a Random Forest ensemble classifier. The primary output is a discrete class label, with reported metrics including accuracy (97.2%), sensitivity, specificity, precision, and AUC (0.99).

A U-Net model is used for semantic segmentation to generate precise binary masks of telangiectasia (blood vessels) within the lesions. This is an intermediate step to extract handcrafted features for the classification task, not the final diagnostic prediction.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available skin lesion dermoscopy dataset containing over 10,000 skin images for seven diagnostic categories. Images used in this study were 8-bit RGB of size 450 × 600.

use.training: Used for training the U-Net model (127 BCC images) and the EfficientNet-B5 model (1000 non-BCC images). Also used for BCC classification training/validation/test splits.
use.validation: Used for validation splits of the BCC and non-BCC datasets.
use.testing: Used for the holdout test set of the BCC classification model.
use.other: Source of ground truth masks for U-Net training (manually drawn and verified by dermatologist).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public skin lesion dataset. Images used in this study were 8-bit RGB of size 450 × 600.

use.training: Used for training the U-Net model (90 BCC images).
use.other: Source of ground truth masks for U-Net training.

datasets_used.name: NIH Study Dataset (CA153927-01 and CA101639-02A2)
datasets_used.description: Data from NIH studies. Images used in this study were 8-bit RGB of size 1024 × 768.

use.training: Used for training the U-Net model (783 BCC images).
use.other: Source of ground truth masks for U-Net training. Patient information is not publicly available.

datasets_used.name: Custom BCC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A curated dataset of 1000 BCC images created by the authors for U-Net training, combining images from HAM10000, ISIC 2019, and NIH. Ground truth binary vessel masks were manually drawn by the team and verified by a dermatologist.

use.training: Used to train the U-Net model for telangiectasia segmentation.
use.validation: Used to validate the U-Net model.
use.testing: Used to test the U-Net model (195 images).

datasets_used.name: Custom Non-BCC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A curated dataset of 1000 non-BCC images selected from the HAM10000 dataset, comprising five benign categories: benign keratosis, nevus, actinic keratosis, dermatofibroma, and vascular lesion. Images were resized to 448 × 448.

use.training: Used to train the EfficientNet-B5 model.
use.validation: Used to validate the EfficientNet-B5 model.

datasets_used.name: BCC vs Non-BCC Classification Dataset
datasets_used.description: The combined dataset used for the final binary classification task, consisting of the custom BCC and non-BCC datasets. Images were resized to 448 × 448.

use.training: Used to train the fusion model (EfficientNet-B5 features + telangiectasia features) via Random Forest classifier.
use.validation: Used to validate the fusion model.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the fusion model on a holdout test set.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To discard noisy lower-importance features and select the most relevant features for the Random Forest classifier.
To evaluate classification performance and calculate metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision.
To assess the diagnostic ability of the model across different threshold settings.
To evaluate the segmentation performance of the U-Net model for telangiectasia detection.
Mentioned as a future direction to account for significant differences; not currently applied in the study.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper explicitly states that it does not include statistical significance tests, noting that the focus in deep learning has been on predictive accuracy and model generalization rather than mean comparison among groups. ANOVA with post hoc tests is mentioned only as a future direction.

BCC_vs_Non-BCC_Classification_Dataset.description: Holdout test set of 395 images (195 BCC, 200 non-BCC) derived from custom BCC and non-BCC datasets.
BCC_vs_Non-BCC_Classification_Dataset.notes: The EfficientNet-B5-FT-Fusion2 model achieved state-of-the-art accuracy. It showed a 1.3% improvement in accuracy, 3.7% in sensitivity, 1.5% in specificity, and 1.5% in precision over the baseline EfficientNet-B5-FT model. Omitting surround features dropped AUC from 0.995 to 0.993.

U_Net_Segmentation_Test_Set.description: Test set of 195 BCC and 200 non-BCC images used to evaluate the U-Net model for telangiectasia segmentation.

U_Net.Mean_Jaccard_Score: Within the variation of human observers
U_Net.notes: Specific numerical Jaccard score not reported, but described as comparable to human observer variability.

U_Net_Segmentation_Test_Set.notes: The U-Net model was able to find vessels in both BCC and non-BCC categories. The segmentation accuracy allowed for the extraction of handcrafted features.

Benchmark_Comparison_Datasets.description: Datasets from other studies used for comparative performance analysis.
Benchmark_Comparison_Datasets.notes: The proposed method (0.972 Accuracy) outperformed Kharazmi et al. (2017) and Kharazmi et al. (2018) in accuracy. It achieved similar AUC to Serrano et al. (2022) but with slightly better accuracy (0.972 vs 0.970) and higher precision (0.965 vs 0.953).

architecture_used.main_model: Fusion of U-Net (for telangiectasia segmentation) and EfficientNet-B5 (for feature extraction), combined with a Random Forest ensemble classifier.
architecture_used.pretraining: EfficientNet-B5 was pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset. U-Net was trained on manually drawn ground truth masks verified by a dermatologist.
architecture_used.input_size: 448 × 448 pixels (resized from original sizes of 450×600 or 1024×768).

segmentation_model.name: U-Net
segmentation_model.purpose: Semantic segmentation of telangiectasia (blood vessels) to generate binary masks.

feature_extractor.name: EfficientNet-B5
feature_extractor.backbone: Mobile Inverted Bottleneck Convolution (MBConv) blocks (MBConv1 and MBConv6).
feature_extractor.structure: 14 stages. Stage 1: Conv 3x3 + BN + Swish. Stages 2-8: MBConv blocks with kernel sizes 3x3 and 5x5. Stage 9: Conv 1x1. Stages 10-11: BN and Softmax activation. Stage 12: Global Average Pooling. Stage 13: Dropout. Stage 14: Dense layer.

architecture_details.fusion_method: Concatenation of deep learning features (from EfficientNet-B5) and handcrafted features (from U-Net masks).

classifier.name: Random Forest
classifier.purpose: Binary classification of BCC vs non-BCC.

training_details.framework: Keras with TensorFlow backend in Python 3.7.
training_details.hardware: Single 32 GB Nvidia V100 graphics card.

classification_or_output_head.feature_selection: Feature importance ranking using average Gini impurity decrease. Top 50 deep learning features (importance > 0.005) and top 23 handcrafted features (importance > 0.01) were selected for the final model (EfficientNet-B5-FT-Fusion2).

preprocessing_techniques: Histogram stretching applied to each color channel
Vessel mask dilation with a 3 × 3 structuring element
Closing vessel masks with a 2 × 2 structuring element
Multiplying vessel masks with U-Net-generated lesion masks to isolate vessels within the lesion
Geometric data augmentation (rotation ±30°, horizontal/vertical flip, width shift ±0.2, height shift ±0.2, shear ±0.2)
Color data augmentation (applied only to U-Net telangiectasia dataset, not to the deep learning feature extraction model)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to both U-Net and EfficientNet models. Included rotation (+30° to -30° in reflect mode), horizontal and vertical flip, width shift (-0.2 to +0.2), height shift (-0.2 to +0.2), and shear (-0.2 to +0.2).

Applied only to the U-Net model for telangiectasia segmentation to address similarity between vessels and red-pigmented skin. Included histogram stretching to each color channel, contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE), normalization, and brightness enhancement.
Applied to U-Net ground truth masks to prevent vessels from breaking during geometric augmentation. Used a 3x3 structuring element for dilation and a 2x2 structuring element for closing.

Augmentation significantly increased training sample counts: U-Net BCC training set expanded from 650 to 3900 images; EfficientNet BCC training set from 650 to 3900 images; EfficientNet Non-BCC training set from 638 to 3828 images.

conclusions: The proposed fusion technique, combining deep learning features from EfficientNet-B5 with handcrafted telangiectasia features segmented by U-Net, achieved state-of-the-art binary classification accuracy of 97.2% for Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) versus non-BCC diagnosis.
The fusion model demonstrated a 1.3% improvement in accuracy, 3.7% in sensitivity, 1.5% in specificity, and 1.5% in precision compared to the deep learning-only EfficientNet-B5 baseline model.
The model achieved an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.995, outperforming benchmark techniques by Kharazmi et al. (2017 and 2018) and showing slightly better accuracy than Serrano et al. (2022).

Incorporating handcrafted features derived from semantically segmented telangiectasia (including surround areas) and discarding noisy lower-importance features via Random Forest feature importance analysis significantly improved diagnostic performance.

The study demonstrates that integrating clinically observable vascular features (telangiectasia) with deep learning enhances both diagnostic accuracy and explainability, addressing the 'black-box' nature of pure deep learning models.

The U-Net model for telangiectasia segmentation achieved a mean Jaccard score within the variation of human observers, validating the reliability of the extracted vessel masks for feature quantification.
The approach confirms the superiority of ensemble learning methods for selecting robust feature combinations to improve model accuracy in dermatological diagnosis.
Further exploration of deep learning techniques for individual dermoscopy feature detection is warranted to expand upon these findings.
important_remarks: The study lacks statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals), relying solely on predictive accuracy metrics, which limits the statistical rigor of the performance claims.
The dataset lacks diversity in skin color, which limits the generalizability of the model to populations with darker skin tones.

Ground truth vessel masks were annotated by a single dermatologist (WVS) with only one team observer per mask, introducing potential inter-annotator bias and limiting the robustness of the segmentation ground truth.

The final sensitivity (0.979) was lower than that reported by Serrano et al. (0.993), although the overall accuracy was higher, indicating a trade-off in diagnostic performance compared to some benchmarks.

The study does not include patient profile information in the classification model, despite acknowledging that such data can aid diagnosis, potentially missing clinically relevant contextual features.
The NIH study dataset used for training contains patient information that is not publicly available, limiting reproducibility and external validation for that specific subset.
The comparison with benchmark studies is complicated by the lack of a shared dataset with ground truths, making direct performance comparisons less definitive.

The model was trained on a relatively small dataset (1000 BCC and 1000 non-BCC images) requiring extensive data augmentation to prevent overfitting, which may affect real-world performance on unseen data distributions.
